NAPA COUNTY
Commission
958L4
Re: Potential for Conflicc of Interest by Industry Members of Local
Task Forces Formed to lEplement the Integrated l,Iaste Managernent
Acr of 19 89
Dear Mr. Morodoni :
As we discussed by telephone last month, NaPa County has recently formed
a Local Task Force pursuant to the lntegrated Waste Management Act of 1989
(Public Resources Code section 40000 et seq.) to assist the cities and county
in the preparation and adoption of the various solid waste Plans and elements
required under the new Act. 
Our office is currently acting as legal advisor
for this group r,rhich currently includes at least one member in the
collection/recycling/composting/landf ill business and one recycling
consultant.
on behalf of this Task Force, I am writing to request an opinion from
your office as to whether the Political Reform Act of 1914 (Government Code
section 81000 et seq.) precludes Task Force members drawn from the private
solid waste industry from meaningful Parcicipation in substantive functions of
the Task Force or whether the "regulated industry" excePtion [2 Cal.Adrnin.Code
section 18703(c) ] will allow such mernbers to continue to Participate as long
as the effect on their individual financial interests is not significantly
different frorn the effect on industry mernbers generally
The problem has arisen because the Integrated Waste Management Act of
1989 does not include the language which prior opinions of your office and the
Cornmission have su8Sested are necessary to invoke the "regulated industry
exception". Legislative declarations in the Act do indicate that decisions
involving the establishment or exPansion of solid waste facilities 
qhpgL!
include ;rneaningful public and private solid waste industry participagion"
fpublic Resources Code section 40900(b) ] and that it is che intent and policy
of th. stut" that each local agency concerned with the solid waste facility
,<ffiA
(-(r*\ )-)
W
ROBERT WESTMEYER,
County Counsol
MARGARET L. WOOOBUBY, Chiet Doputy
JOSEPH C. FOLKAnD, Deputy
DAViD L. ZALTSMAN, O€puty
NANCY STUART GRISHAM, Deputy
KEVIN STOBAUGH, Doputy
October 16, 19 90
Mark Morodomi
Legal Department

428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California

TY COUNSEL
1195 THIRD STREET, ROOM 301 . NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94559'3001
AREA CODE 707 1253-4521

Mark Morodomi
October 16, 19 90

planning and siting process " involve" the private solid rraste industry IPublic
Resources Code Section 40900(d)]. Holrever, the Act does not use such
mandatory language in relation to industry nenbership on the Task Force as
would unambiguously trigger application of the "regulated industry" exception,
but instead uses the words "44y include" [Public Resources Code section
40950(b)]. In additsion, there is no express reference to the Political 
Reform
Act of l9l4 in the provisions of the Integrated Waste Managemen! Act of 1989
relating to Local Task Forces.
Yet, if use of the word "may" and lack of reference to the conflicts
statutes means that the "regulated industry" exception does not apply, it is
1ike1y that application of the usual confl.ict regulatlons would bar an
"industry" mernber from participating in nosE, if not all, of the subsEanEive
duties of lhe Task Force. Phococopies of those portions of the 1989 Act and
accorapanying regulations secring forth the substantive duties of the Local
Task Forces are attached for your convenience.
If this is the case, the question naturally arises why the Act would
even bother to authorize the Board of Supervlsors to appolnt industry members,
since the net effecE could be to not only prohibit deliberation and voting by
such persons as Task Force mernbers but also to silence them as witnesses
before the Task Porce unless they happen to be sole owners of lheir
businesses. In a snall county such as Napa Ehis could have a chilling effect
clearly contrary to the declarations of legislative intent sunmarized above.
Advice frorn your office at the earliest possible date regarding this
issue will be greatly appreciated, since the Task Porce will be occupied with
occupied with non-substantive organizational and educational activities only
for the first few meetings.
S incerely ,

sections.question: whether the Political Reform Act of 1974 (Government Code section 81000 et seq.) precludes Task Force members drawn from the private solid waste industry from meaningful participation in substantive functions of the Task Force or whether the "regulated industry" exception [2 Cal.Admin.Code section 18703(c)] will allow such members to continue to participate as long as the effect on their individual financial interests is not significantly different from the effect on industry members generally
sections.question_synthetic: Under the Political Reform Act of 1974, can private solid waste industry members meaningfully participate in substantive functions of a local task force established under the Integrated Waste Management Act of 1989, or does the "regulated industry" exception apply to permit their participation?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from Napa County to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion section exists. Document discusses conflict of interest concerns regarding industry representation on local task forces under the 1989 Integrated Waste Management Act. Document ID 90665 appears to reference the request number.

embedding.summary: Napa County's Chief Deputy County Counsel requests FPPC guidance on whether private solid waste industry members can participate in substantive Task Force functions without violating conflict of interest provisions, given the Integrated Waste Management Act's legislative intent to include industry participation but lack of explicit reference to the regulated industry exception.
